stories.title: The Bermuda Specialist
stories.intended_reader: Those who sailed Monarch during her Caribbean and Bermuda years
stories.core_insight: Monarch specialized in Bermuda cruises and Caribbean variety, serving multiple homeports
stories.markdown:
**I can still hear the foghorn. Two in the morning, somewhere off Cape Hatteras, and Monarch of the Seas was plowing north through a bank of fog so thick the running lights couldn't cut it. I stood on my balcony in my bathrobe and listened to the horn pulse into the dark, and I thought: this is what it feels like to be heading somewhere that matters.**

We were three days from Bermuda. My wife Ellen and I had booked Monarch's summer Bermuda run out of Cape Liberty—Bayonne, New Jersey, for those who know it by its street address rather than its maritime ambition. We'd chosen Monarch over newer ships because the itinerary gave us something no other Royal Caribbean vessel offered from the Northeast: three full days docked at Kings Wharf, Bermuda.

Three days. Not a few hours in port with the clock ticking. Three complete days when the ship became a hotel and the island became your neighborhood.

Monarch launched in November 1991 as the second Sovereign-class ship, and while her sister [Sovereign](/ships/rcl/sovereign-of-the-seas.html) focused on short Bahamas loops and [Majesty](/ships/rcl/majesty-of-the-seas.html) followed the same pattern, Monarch carved a different path. She became Royal Caribbean's Bermuda specialist—summer seasons from East Coast ports, sailing routes that gave passengers a relationship with the island rather than a glimpse of it.

At 73,937 gross tons and 2,354 passengers, Monarch was a genuine mega-ship by the standards of her era. The five-story atrium with glass elevators made the interior feel grander than her tonnage suggested. The two-deck dining room hosted dinners that felt event-like, especially on formal nights when the whole ship dressed up and the waiters brought the baked Alaska in a parade.

But what made Monarch special wasn't her specifications. It was her calendar.

Every summer, like clockwork, Monarch repositioned to the Northeast and began her Bermuda runs. Families from New York, New Jersey, Connecticut, and Pennsylvania could drive to Bayonne—no flights, no airports, no TSA lines—and board a cruise ship bound for pink sand beaches and pastel houses. The drive-to accessibility changed everything for families who couldn't afford or stomach the logistics of flying to Florida.

Ellen and I discovered this in 2005, our first Monarch Bermuda sailing. We drove from our home in northern New Jersey to Bayonne in forty minutes. Forty minutes from our driveway to the gangway. The simplicity of it felt revolutionary.

Bermuda passengers developed fierce loyalty to Monarch. Not just to the ship, but to the ritual: summer Bermuda week, the same ship, often the same cabin category, always the same island waiting at the end. I met couples who'd sailed Monarch to Bermuda eight, ten, twelve times. They knew Kings Wharf like their own neighborhood. They had favorite restaurants in Hamilton. They could navigate St. George's without a map.

Our three days docked at Kings Wharf became the template for every Bermuda trip after. We'd walk to Horseshoe Bay on the first morning—early, before the tour buses, when the pink sand was still cool. We'd explore Hamilton on the second day, browsing shops and eating fish sandwiches on the harbor. We'd save St. George's for the third day, wandering the oldest continuously inhabited English settlement in the Western Hemisphere.

And every evening, we'd walk back to Monarch. She'd be there, lit up at the dock, a floating hotel that never charged us for the room. Dinner aboard, drinks in the lounge, sleep with the porthole cracked to let in the Bermuda air.

When Royal Caribbean transferred Monarch to Pullmantur in 2013, the Bermuda Facebook groups erupted. People mourned like they'd lost a family member. They shared photographs, sailing dates, crew names, favorite memories. A woman posted a picture of her daughter standing on Monarch's deck at age three, and beside it a photo of the same daughter at twenty-one, same deck, same railing, same smile.

I still think about that foghorn off Hatteras. The sound of a ship heading north through the dark toward an island that waited like an old friend. Monarch carried thousands of families on that passage, summer after summer, year after year, until the passage belonged to memory and the ship belonged to history.

**The lesson:** Some ships find their purpose not in being the biggest or newest, but in being the faithful one—the one that shows up every summer, in the same waters, carrying the same families home.

stories.title: The Ship Loyal Passengers Defended
stories.intended_reader: Monarch loyalists who sailed her repeatedly
stories.core_insight: Monarch inspired unusual passenger loyalty despite being an older ship
stories.markdown:
**I found the thread on a cruise forum in 2011, two years before Monarch left Royal Caribbean, and the title said it all: "In Defense of Monarch of the Seas."**

Someone had posted dismissively about the ship—old, outdated, lacking the flashy amenities of Oasis class. Within hours, fifty-three responses had piled up. Not angry responses. Loving ones. People writing paragraphs about a cruise ship the way you'd write about a person who'd been underestimated.

"Sailed Monarch to Bermuda eight times," wrote a user from Long Island. "Same ship, same route, never got old. You want to know why? Because Monarch was never about the ship. She was about what happened on the ship. And what happened was this: the crew remembered our names, the island waited every summer, and for seven days we were the best version of our family."

I started saving these testimonials. Not because anyone asked me to, but because I'm a journalist by trade, and I recognized what I was reading: an artifact of loyalty so specific it could only be earned, never manufactured.

The stories shared common threads. Crew retention came up constantly. Monarch kept her crew for years—a rarity in an industry where crew contracts are typically short-term and assignments rotate. Multiple passengers mentioned waiters, bartenders, and cabin stewards by name, describing relationships that spanned five, ten, even fifteen years of annual sailings.

A woman from Baltimore described a waiter named Angelo who served her family for eleven consecutive Bermuda cruises. "He watched my sons grow up," she wrote. "He knew their favorite desserts, their allergies, their birthdays. When my older boy got into college, Angelo brought a cake to our table. He'd arranged it with the galley himself. Where else does that happen?"

A retired firefighter from Queens posted a photograph of himself and his wife on Monarch's deck in 1993, and beside it an identical photo from 2012. Same couple, same railing, nineteen years between them. "We're grayer now," he wrote. "But Monarch is the same. That's what we love. Everything else changes. Monarch doesn't."

Except Monarch did change. Or rather, the fleet changed around her.

By the mid-2000s, [Voyager class](/ships/rcl/voyager-of-the-seas.html) had introduced the Royal Promenade. [Oasis class](/ships/rcl/oasis-of-the-seas.html) had brought Central Park to sea. [Quantum class](/ships/rcl/quantum-of-the-seas.html) was on the horizon with North Star and robot bartenders. Monarch, with her five-story atrium and her traditional dining room and her unflashy reliability, looked increasingly like a ship from another era.

Which, of course, she was.

But the forum thread revealed something the fleet strategy couldn't account for: passengers who didn't want the newest era. Who chose Monarch precisely because she was from another time. Who found in her consistency a kind of comfort that innovation, by definition, cannot provide.

When Royal Caribbean announced Monarch's transfer to Pullmantur in 2013, the forums went into mourning. People posted farewell messages addressed directly to the ship. "Dear Monarch," began one, "you held my family for twenty years. You were the one constant in our summers. I know you're just steel and engines, but you felt like more. You felt like the place where we were most ourselves."

I printed that one out. It's pinned to the corkboard above my desk.

Monarch was renamed and sailed for Pullmantur until the company ceased operations in 2020. She was scrapped in Alang, India that same year. The steel was recycled. The engines were dismantled. The five-story atrium that hosted twenty years of formal-night parades was cut into pieces and melted down.

But the thread still exists. Fifty-three responses defending a ship that needed no defense, written by people who understood that loyalty isn't about features or tonnage or the year a ship was built. It's about showing up. Year after year, in the same waters, with the same warmth, until the passengers and the ship become part of each other's story.

**The lesson:** Loyalty isn't earned by being the best. It's earned by being faithful.

stories.title: Family Cruising in the 1990s
stories.intended_reader: Families who sailed Monarch during the 1990s and early 2000s
stories.core_insight: Monarch provided accessible family cruising before the mega-ship era
stories.markdown:
**My mother has a rule about vacation photographs: you take one on the first day and one on the last day, and the difference between them tells you whether the trip was worth it.**

The first-day photo from our 1996 Monarch of the Seas cruise shows a family barely holding together. My father's jaw is clenched. My mother's smile doesn't reach her eyes. My brother and I are standing slightly apart, the way siblings do when they've been fighting in the car for six hours. We'd driven from our house in Connecticut to Bayonne in what should have been a four-hour trip but turned into six because of traffic on the George Washington Bridge and a rest stop incident involving my brother, a Slurpee, and the back of my father's neck.

The last-day photo shows a different family. My father's arm is around my mother. She's leaning into him. My brother and I are sunburned, grinning, standing shoulder to shoulder with the easy intimacy of kids who've spent a week being happy together. Behind us, Monarch rises like a white cliff, her funnel catching the morning sun.

Something happened between those photographs, and I've spent thirty years trying to name it.

Family cruising in the 1990s was different from what it is now. There were no waterslides taller than buildings. No robot bartenders or virtual reality arcades or surf simulators. What there was, on Monarch, was space. Deck space where kids could run without colliding with strangers every ten feet. Pool space where families could actually swim rather than just stand in crowded water. Dining space where assigned seating meant you sat with the same people every evening and built something resembling a temporary community.

The youth programs divided kids by age, which meant my brother and I were mercifully separated. He was ten and joined the older group. I was seven and joined the younger one. We spent our days in parallel universes—separate activities, separate adventures, separate stories to tell at dinner.

That separation was the first gift. At home, my brother and I were in constant competition for attention, space, and parental approval. On Monarch, we had our own territories. The competition dissolved because there was nothing to compete for. When we met at the pool in the afternoon, we were genuinely glad to see each other. That hadn't happened at home in months.

My parents got something too. They'd been married fifteen years by then, and the marriage was in that middle place—not bad, not great, just running on autopilot. The ship gave them evenings. After we were deposited in the youth programs after dinner, they had three hours to themselves. I didn't know this at the time, of course. I learned it years later when my mother told me that Monarch was where she and my father remembered how to talk to each other.

"We'd go to the lounge," she said. "Just the two of us. And we'd talk about things that weren't logistics. Not who's picking up the kids, not when's the mortgage due, not what's for dinner. We talked about memories. Plans. Dreams we'd forgotten we had."

The Bermuda itinerary gave us three full days docked at Kings Wharf, and those days felt like vacation within a vacation. We walked to Horseshoe Bay as a family. My brother and I built sandcastles while my parents sat on towels and read books and looked, in my seven-year-old memory, like people who actually enjoyed each other's company.

I didn't understand then what Monarch was doing for us. I thought it was just a big boat with a pool and unlimited ice cream. I didn't know that the ship's design—the youth programs, the adult lounges, the Bermuda calendar that gave families time instead of rushing them—was quietly restructuring our family's dynamics.

But those two photographs tell the story. First day: a family strung tight with the tension of ordinary life. Last day: the same family, loosened, reconnected, leaning into each other instead of pulling apart.

We sailed Monarch twice more in the late 1990s. By the time I was a teenager, I was too cool for family cruises. But I carried those Bermuda weeks with me into adulthood, and when I had my own children, I knew exactly what I wanted to give them: a ship, an island, a week of space to become the family we were meant to be.

**The lesson:** The best family vacations don't fix anything. They just clear enough space for a family to find each other again.

stories.title: West Coast Repositioning
stories.intended_reader: West Coast cruisers who sailed Monarch's Mexican Riviera itineraries
stories.core_insight: Monarch successfully served both East and West Coast markets
stories.markdown:
**I found out Royal Caribbean was sending a ship to Los Angeles the way most Southern Californians find out about things: someone mentioned it at a barbecue, casually, between bites of tri-tip, like they were sharing a weather forecast instead of information that would change my family's vacation habits for five years.**

"Monarch of the Seas," my neighbor Dave said. "Seven-night Mexican Riviera. Out of San Pedro." He shrugged. "We're thinking of trying it."

I'd been wanting to cruise for years. The problem was geography. We lived in Pasadena, and every cruise I'd researched left from Florida or the Northeast. That meant flights—flights for four, which in our family's budget arithmetic meant the cruise vacation never made it past the "someday" column.

But San Pedro was forty-five minutes from our driveway. Forty-five minutes from the garage to the gangway. No flights. No airports. No luggage carousels.

We booked Monarch's Mexican Riviera itinerary for March 2009, and the moment we drove into the port and saw the ship—white hull, Royal Caribbean blue, rising above the industrial landscape of the LA harbor like a misplaced luxury hotel—I knew something had shifted.

Monarch wasn't the newest ship in the fleet. By 2009, she was eighteen years old, and if you'd sailed the newer vessels, you could see it. The cabins were compact. The entertainment was traditional rather than spectacular. The pool deck was functional, not dramatic. She didn't have a Royal Promenade or a FlowRider or a Central Park.

What she had was the Pacific Ocean and seven nights to explore it.

Cabo San Lucas hit us first. The arch at Land's End, visible from the tender as we approached, looked exactly like the photographs, which is something you can never quite believe until you see it yourself. We walked the marina, ate tacos at a place the crew had recommended, and watched our kids splash in water so clear it barely looked like water at all.

Mazatlan was louder, messier, more authentically Mexican. We took a pulmonia—a small open-air taxi that felt like a golf cart with ambitions—through the old town, past the cathedral, past markets where vendors sold fruit I couldn't name and my wife bought a ceramic sun for our garden that still hangs there today.

Puerto Vallarta was the one that caught us off guard. We'd expected tourist infrastructure, and we found it, but we also found the malecon—the seaside boardwalk where artists sell sculptures and musicians play for tips and the sunset turns the sky colors that shouldn't exist outside of paintings. Our daughter, who was nine, stood on the malecon and said, "I didn't know the world was like this." That sentence, from a nine-year-old, is worth more than any review I could write.

Monarch's Los Angeles deployment was strategic brilliance disguised as fleet management. At a time when most Royal Caribbean ships served Caribbean and European markets, sending Monarch west filled a gap. West Coast passengers—millions of potential cruisers in Southern California, Arizona, and Nevada—had limited options. Carnival and Princess dominated. Royal Caribbean was barely a presence.

Monarch changed that. She wasn't the newest or flashiest ship, but she was *available*. She proved that West Coast markets would support Royal Caribbean's brand. The pricing was competitive. The itinerary was compelling. The drive-to accessibility from Southern California eliminated the flight barrier that had kept families like mine on shore.

We sailed Monarch's Mexican Riviera four more times between 2009 and 2012. Same ship, same ports, same drive to San Pedro. Each trip, the ship felt more like ours—a West Coast ritual in a line that had always been an East Coast brand.

When Monarch left Los Angeles in 2012 and was transferred to Pullmantur, the loss stung. But the seed she planted grew. Later, newer ships—[Navigator of the Seas](/ships/rcl/navigator-of-the-seas.html) and eventually Quantum-class vessels—would serve West Coast homeports, building on the loyalty and market validation that Monarch had quietly established.

She paved the way. That's her West Coast legacy. Not the flashiest ship, not the newest, but the first Royal Caribbean vessel that made a Pasadena family feel like the cruise life wasn't just for people who lived near Port Canaveral.

**The lesson:** Sometimes the greatest gift isn't the grandest ship. It's the one that shows up where you live and says, "The ocean is closer than you think."
